title: Opera
intro: Opera questions span composers, individual works, arias, singers, and terminology. Composer answerlines ask for the figures who defined national schools and eras — Monteverdi, Purcell, and Handel in the Baroque, Rameau and Gluck bridging into Mozart's Classical era, Rossini through Verdi and Puccini in 19th- and early 20th-century Italy, Meyerbeer, Bizet, Massenet, and Offenbach in France, Wagner, Strauss, and Berg in the German tradition, Mussorgsky and Smetana further east, and Sullivan, Britten, Glass, Adams, and Adès in English-language opera. Individual-opera answerlines cover plot, characters, and famous excerpts (Rigoletto, Carmen, Tristan und Isolde), while a smaller set of answers names specific arias ("Vesti la giubba," "Nessun dorma"), singers (Maria Callas), or concepts (verismo, castrato, patter song).

The canon is dominated by Italian opera: Verdi and Puccini are the two most-asked composers, and their signature works — La Traviata, Rigoletto, Aida, La bohème, Tosca, Turandot — recur throughout the bel canto and verismo sections, alongside deeper cuts like Verdi's Nabucco, Il trovatore, and Otello and Puccini's Manon Lescaut. Mozart is the leading Classical-era composer, his three collaborations with librettist Da Ponte (The Marriage of Figaro, Don Giovanni, Così fan tutte) appearing more often than his German Singspiele or the early opera seria Idomeneo. Bel canto composers Rossini, Bellini, and Donizetti form a distinct cluster bridging Classical and Romantic Italy, each represented by both a signature tragedy and a lighter comedy, while the verismo generation — Puccini, plus the single-opera answers Cavalleria Rusticana, I Pagliacci, La Gioconda, and La Wally — marks Italian opera's turn toward realism.

Beyond Italy, answers cluster by national tradition. French opera centers on Bizet's Carmen and the Grand Opera spectacle of Meyerbeer's Les Huguenots, with Gounod's Faust and Roméo et Juliette, Massenet's Thaïs, and Offenbach's The Tales of Hoffmann rounding out the group. German-language opera is anchored by Wagner's Ring cycle and other music dramas and Richard Strauss's psychologically intense scores, with Beethoven's sole opera Fidelio, Berg's atonal Wozzeck, and Zimmermann's Die Soldaten marking its 20th-century edge.

A newer cluster of national traditions rounds out the field. Russian and Eastern European answers include Mussorgsky's Boris Godunov, Smetana's Czech nationalism, Dvořák's Rusalka, and Prokofiev's The Love for Three Oranges. English-language opera runs from Gilbert and Sullivan's Victorian operettas through Gershwin's Porgy and Bess and Menotti's Amahl and the Night Visitors to the 20th-century figures Britten, Glass, and Adams and the contemporary Thomas Adès. Throughout, castrato, Maria Callas, coloratura soprano, patter song, sleepwalking, and specific arias represent the category's attention to performance practice and vocal terminology alongside composers and works.

sections.name: Baroque & Classical Opera (1600-1800)
sections.blurb: Opera's earliest century, from Monteverdi's sung drama through Handel's opera seria to Mozart's synthesis of comedy and myth.

entries.note: Early Baroque composer of L'Orfeo (1607), among the first operas still regularly performed; helped establish recitative-based drama.
English Baroque composer whose sole full-length opera, written for a girls' school, drew on Virgil's Aeneid.

works.note: Short opera of the Carthaginian queen abandoned by Aeneas; her lament "When I am laid in earth" closes it.

entries.note: Baroque composer of Italian opera seria, including Giulio Cesare and Rinaldo, written for star castrato singers.
French Baroque composer-theorist whose tragedies en musique, built on Lully's model, added richer harmony and orchestration.
Reform-era composer of Orfeo ed Euridice; stripped away vocal ornamentation to prioritize dramatic clarity over display.
Common-link answerline spanning Monteverdi's L'Orfeo, Gluck's Orfeo ed Euridice, and Offenbach's Orpheus in the Underworld.
Classical composer of Don Giovanni, The Marriage of Figaro, and The Magic Flute, spanning opera seria and comedy.

works.note: Opera of the seducer dragged to hell by the murdered Commendatore's statue; includes "Là ci darem la mano."
Singspiel of Tamino's fairy-tale trials and Masonic symbolism; the Queen of the Night's aria demands an extreme high F.
Comedy of the servants Figaro and Susanna outwitting the philandering Count; sequel to Rossini's Barber of Seville.
Singspiel of a rescue from a Turkish harem; spoken German dialogue alternates with virtuosic arias.
Two soldiers, urged by a cynical philosopher, test their fiancées' fidelity by wooing each other's partner in disguise.
Early opera seria in which the Cretan king must sacrifice his son to fulfill a vow to Neptune.

entries.note: Philandering Count of Mozart's Marriage of Figaro, seen earlier as a young suitor of Rosina in Rossini's prequel Barber of Seville.
Beaumarchais's clever barber-valet, hero of both Rossini's Barber of Seville and its Mozart-composed sequel The Marriage of Figaro.
German-language genre alternating spoken dialogue with song, as in Mozart's Abduction from the Seraglio and The Magic Flute.

sections.name: Bel Canto & Verdi (Italian Romantic Opera)
sections.blurb: Italy's early-to-mid Romantic century, bel canto's florid vocal display giving way to Verdi's tighter musical drama.

entries.note: Early Romantic composer of The Barber of Seville and other bel canto comedies; known for rapid-fire patter arias.

works.note: Comedy of the barber Figaro aiding Count Almaviva's courtship of Rosina; precedes Marriage of Figaro's plot.

entries.note: Prolific bel canto composer of Lucia di Lammermoor; also wrote comedies such as The Elixir of Love and Don Pasquale.

works.note: Opera of a bride forced into marriage who murders her husband and descends into madness in the Mad Scene.
Comedy of Marie, a vivandière raised by a French army regiment; the tenor's aria demands nine high Cs.
Comedy in which an elderly bachelor's sham marriage to the scheming young widow Norina teaches him a humbling lesson.

entries.note: Bel canto composer of Norma; wrote long, ornamented melodic lines for dramatic sopranos.

works.note: Opera of a Druid priestess torn between duty and a forbidden Roman proconsul; "Casta diva" is her signature aria.
Final opera, set amid the English Civil War; the heroine Elvira's mad scene echoes Lucia's, though this one ends happily.

entries.note: Mid-Romantic composer of Rigoletto, La Traviata, and Aida; tightened Italian opera's drama and psychological focus.

works.note: Opera of the courtesan Violetta, who sacrifices love for her lover's family honor and dies of consumption.
Opera of a court jester whose curse dooms his daughter Gilda, killed in his place.
Rigoletto's aria for the philandering Duke of Mantua, cynically insisting that women are the fickle ones.
Opera of a captured Nubian princess loved by an Egyptian general; both are entombed alive at its close.
Rival half-brothers, the troubadour Manrico and Count di Luna, both love Leonora; features the Anvil Chorus and a gypsy's revenge.
Late tragedy after Shakespeare; the Moorish general is manipulated by the ensign Iago into murdering his wife Desdemona.
Historical opera of the Spanish prince, his father Philip II, and the auto-da-fé staged by the Grand Inquisitor.
Final opera, a comedy after Shakespeare's Merry Wives of Windsor centered on the fat knight's schemes and eventual humiliation.
Early opera on the Babylonian captivity of the Jews; its chorus "Va, pensiero" became an Italian nationalist anthem.

sections.name: Verismo & Puccini (Italian Opera, 1890s-1920s)
sections.blurb: Late 19th-century Italy turns from Romantic grandeur to gritty realism, with Puccini as its dominant voice.

entries.note: Late 19th-century Italian realist movement depicting ordinary, often violent lives; exemplified by Cavalleria Rusticana and I Pagliacci.
Verismo-era composer of La bohème, Tosca, and Madama Butterfly; Turandot left unfinished at his death.

works.note: Unfinished opera of an icy Chinese princess who poses riddles to suitors; contains the aria "Nessun dorma."
Prince Calaf's aria from Turandot, vowing that no one shall sleep until he wins Turandot's hand by dawn.
Opera of poor Parisian artists; the seamstress Mimì dies of consumption after reuniting with Rodolfo.
Opera of the singer Tosca, who kills the police chief Scarpia then leaps to her death after her lover's execution.
Opera of Cio-Cio-San, a Nagasaki geisha abandoned by the American officer Pinkerton; ends in her suicide.
Early opera after Prévost's novel; the heroine Manon dies in a Louisiana desert after her lover Des Grieux's ruin.

works.topic: The Girl of the Golden West
works.answerline: The Girl of the Golden West
works.note: Opera set in a California Gold Rush mining camp; the sheriff Rance loses saloon-keeper Minnie to the outlaw Ramerrez.

entries.note: Mascagni's one-act verismo opera of Sicilian village honor and a fatal duel; frequently double-billed with I Pagliacci.
Leoncavallo's verismo opera in which the clown Canio murders his unfaithful wife Nedda onstage during a performance.

works.note: Canio's aria, sung while the clown weeps donning his costume before performing despite his heartbreak.

entries.note: Giordano's opera set in the French Revolution; the poet Andrea Chénier and Maddalena choose the guillotine together.
Ponchielli's opera of a Venetian street singer's self-sacrifice for her rival in love; its ballet "Dance of the Hours" is widely excerpted.
Catalani's opera of a stubborn Tyrolean woman whose farewell aria "Ebben? Ne andrò lontana" precedes a fatal avalanche.

sections.name: French Opera (19th Century)
sections.blurb: French opera balances Grand Opera spectacle with lighter lyric and opéra comique traditions.

entries.note: Grand Opera composer of Les Huguenots; built spectacle from massed chorus, ballet, and historical pageantry.

works.note: Five-act Grand Opera set amid the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre of French Protestants.

entries.note: Composer of Carmen, his only lasting success; died months after its lukewarm premiere.

works.note: Opera of the Seville cigarette girl who leaves Don José for Escamillo; Habanera, Toreador Song.
Earlier opera set among Ceylonese pearl divers; friends Zurga and Nadir both love the priestess Leïla, "Au fond du temple saint."

entries.note: Lyric composer of Manon and Werther, alongside the frequently cited Thaïs; known for sensuous orchestration.

works.note: Opera of a courtesan converted to piety by the monk Athanaël, who instead falls in love with her; contains the orchestral "Méditation."

entries.note: Composer of French operetta and opéra bouffe; his sole true opera adapted E.T.A. Hoffmann's fantastic tales.

works.note: The poet Hoffmann recounts three failed loves involving a mechanical doll, a courtesan, and a consumptive singer.

entries.note: Gounod's opera after Goethe; the scholar Faust sells his soul to Méphistophélès to seduce Marguerite, who is ultimately redeemed.
Gounod's other enduring opera, after Shakespeare; adds a final duet in which the lovers briefly wake before dying.

sections.name: German & Austro-Hungarian Opera (Wagner and Successors)
sections.blurb: German-language opera runs from Mozart's successors through Wagner's music dramas to 20th-century atonality.

entries.note: Composer-theorist of Tristan und Isolde, Parsifal, and the Ring cycle; pioneered leitmotif and continuous "music drama."

works.note: Opera of an adulterous love potion and its unresolved "Tristan chord," a landmark of chromatic harmony.
Final opera; the "pure fool" Parsifal redeems the Grail knights and heals the wounded king Amfortas.
Opera of a mysterious knight who rescues Elsa but must leave when she asks his forbidden name; source of the Bridal Chorus.
Opening opera of the Ring cycle; the dwarf Alberich forges a world-dominating ring after renouncing love.
Third Ring opera; the fearless hero reforges his father's sword, slays the dragon Fafner, and wakes the sleeping Brünnhilde.
Bavarian town where Wagner built his own festival theater, designed for the Ring cycle's premiere and still hosting his works.

entries.note: Late-Romantic composer of Salome and Elektra's dissonant intensity, followed by the lyrical Der Rosenkavalier.

works.note: Opera in which the princess demands John the Baptist's head after her Dance of the Seven Veils.
Comedy of the aging Marschallin, who gives up her young lover Octavian to the younger Sophie.
Opera-within-an-opera in which a serious mythological drama and a comic troupe are forced to perform simultaneously.

entries.note: Beethoven's only opera; Leonore disguises herself as a man to free her imprisoned husband Florestan from a political jail.
Johann Strauss II's Viennese operetta of a masked-ball revenge prank and mistaken identities.
Second Viennese School composer whose atonal, expressionist operas set socially critical texts.

works.note: Atonal opera in which an abused soldier murders his lover Marie, then drowns retrieving the knife afterward.

entries.note: Common link: Zimmermann's nearly unstageable Die Soldaten and the disguised soldiers testing their fiancées in Così fan tutte.
Weill and Brecht's satire of the criminal Mackie Messer ("Mack the Knife"); blends jazz-inflected songs with Marxist critique.
Austrian capital central to German-language opera: premiere city of Mozart's and Beethoven's operas and home to Strauss II's operettas.

sections.name: Russian & Eastern European Opera
sections.blurb: A smaller but growing cluster of national schools east of the German tradition.

entries.note: Russian nationalist composer whose stark, folk-inflected harmony shaped his sole completed opera on a guilt-haunted tsar.

works.note: Russian historical opera on the guilt-haunted tsar following the death of the child heir Dmitri.

entries.note: Czech nationalist composer often called the father of Czech opera; best known for the comedy The Bartered Bride.
Dvořák's opera of a water nymph who trades her voice for legs to love a mortal prince; her aria is the "Song to the Moon."
Prokofiev's satirical fairy-tale opera in which a melancholic prince must recover three enchanted oranges, each holding a princess.
Country common link: Bartók's symbolist Duke Bluebeard's Castle and Ligeti's absurdist Le Grand Macabre anchor its questions.

sections.name: English-Language & American Opera
sections.blurb: Victorian operetta through 20th-century minimalism, unified by English-language libretti.

entries.note: Victorian composer, with librettist W. S. Gilbert, of comic operettas built on witty wordplay and tuneful patter songs.

works.topic: The Pirates of Penzance; or, The Slave of Duty
works.answerline: The Pirates of Penzance; or, The Slave of Duty
works.note: Comedy of Frederic, apprenticed to a band of tender-hearted pirates until his 21st birthday, misdated by a leap-year technicality.
Character whose rapid-fire "modern major-general" song catalogues his encyclopedic, impractical learning.

entries.note: Gershwin's opera set in Catfish Row, Charleston; the disabled Porgy and Bess's romance amid Sportin' Life's temptations; "Summertime."
20th-century British composer of Peter Grimes and Billy Budd; explored outsiders through orchestral sea interludes.

works.note: Opera of an outcast Suffolk fisherman blamed for his apprentices' deaths; framed by the orchestral Sea Interludes.

entries.note: Menotti's opera, written for television, in which a disabled boy and his mother host the three Magi en route to Bethlehem.
Minimalist composer of Einstein on the Beach; built from repetitive structures in collaboration with director Robert Wilson.

works.note: Four-and-a-half-hour opera with Robert Wilson; abstract imagery and repetition replace conventional plot and dialogue.

entries.note: Minimalist composer after Glass; Nixon in China and Doctor Atomic dramatize recent history in a documentary style.

works.note: Opera on physicist J. Robert Oppenheimer and the tense final hours before the first atomic bomb test at Trinity.
Opera dramatizing President Nixon's 1972 diplomatic visit, mixing ceremony with the leaders' interior monologues.

entries.note: Contemporary British composer known for dense orchestration in operas including Powder Her Face and The Tempest.

sections.name: Singers, Arias & Vocal Terms
sections.blurb: Cutting across composers and works: the voice types, techniques, and performers that define opera singing.

entries.note: Castrated male singer retaining a high voice; dominated Baroque and Classical opera seria roles, including many of Handel's, until 19th-century decline.
Highest standard female voice type, typically cast as opera's heroines and love interests, from Mozart's Countess to Puccini's Mimì.
Standard high male voice type, typically cast as opera's romantic leads, from Rodolfo in La bohème to the Duke in Rigoletto.
Soprano subtype specializing in rapid runs, trills, and high extension, as required by the Queen of the Night and Lucia's Mad Scene.
Comic aria delivered in rapid-fire syllables, a bel canto device of Rossini's later perfected by Gilbert and Sullivan's operettas.
Plot device in which a guilt-stricken character reveals hidden crimes while asleep, as in Verdi's Macbeth and Bellini's La sonnambula.
Mid-20th-century soprano known for dramatic intensity in bel canto revival roles such as Bellini's Norma.

appendix.answer: "Der Holle Rache kocht in meinem Herzen"
Cosi fan tutte, ossia La scuola degli amanti
Fire Shut Up in My Bones
Rienzi, the Last of the Tribunes
W. S. Gilbert AND Arthur Sullivan
